content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Krishan Chopra 
Senior Assistant City Attorney 
Office of the City Attorney 
P.O. Box 7540 
Mountain View, CA 94039-7540 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-139 
Dear Mr. Chopra: 
August 12, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Mountain View 
Environmental Planning Commissioner Lisa Matichak regarding the conflict of interest provisions 
of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair 
Political Practices Commission (the ''Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering 
advice. (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in governmental decisions relating 
to the City's proposed amendment of its General Plan and adoption of the East Whisman Precise 
Plan given that the Commissioner owns her residence located 1,005 feet from the Precise Plan area? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in those decisions because 
they will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real property financial 
interest in her residence. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Govemmenl Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. 




FACTS 
File No. A-16-139 
Page No. 2 
You are a Senior Assistant City Attorney for the City of Mountain View and the authorized 
representative of Environmental Planning Commissioner Lisa Matichak. The City will soon 
consider whether to amend its General Plan and whether to adopt the East Whisman Precise Plan. 
The East Whisman Precise Plan (the "Precise Plan") is a planning document that would 
modify zoning and design standards for future development within the East Whisman Change Area 
(the "Precise Plan area"), an area identified in the City's existing General Plan. The Precise Plan 
area is approximately 403 acres in size. The existing land uses within the Precise Plan area are 
primarily office and light industrial, with scattered commercial and retail uses. 
The City's recently adopted General Plan authorized an increase of 1.1 million net new 
square feet of commercial office and 27,000 square feet of retail uses, but did not authorize any 
residential uses, within the proposed Precise Plan area. 
The proposed General Plan amendment and Precise Plan adoption would authorize within 
the Precise Plan area the addition of residential uses, an increase in the permitted office "floor area 
ratio" (the ratio of square footage of a building relative to the size of the parcel of land on which it 
sits), and an increase in the permitted intensity of commercial uses. Over time, with ensuing 
development project approvals, these proposed authorizations are likely to result in increased traffic 
levels and intensity of use within the Precise Plan area. The City estimates, if the General Plan is 
amended and the Precise Plan is adopted, that approximately 5,000 multi-family units could be 
added throughout the Precise Plan area, and anticipates that increased office floor area ratio within 
the Precise Plan area will result in higher density commercial development there. 
The Precise Plan contains mitigation measures designed to minimize any traffic increases 
resultant from new development within the Precise Plan area. Among other things, the proposed 
Precise Plan would: require the incorporation of parking standards, comprehensive street planning, 
and multi-modal transportation planning; require the implementation of Transportation Demand 
Management strategies to reduce peak-hour vehicle trips, including regular monitoring, reporting, 
and penalties for noncompliance; and encourage the use of alternatives to motor-vehicle transit, 
such as walking, biking, and light rail. 
The Precise Plan is in the "early visioning process," and the City's Environmental Planning 
Commission is scheduled to consider it for the first time in a study session on August 24, 2016. In 
order to adopt the Precise Plan, the City is expected to undertake the following associated actions: 
• 
General Plan Text and Map Amendment. The General Plan will be amended to identify the 
addition of residential uses, increased office floor area ratio, and/or increased commercial 
intensity within the Precise Plan area. 
• Precise Plan Zoning and Zoning Map Amendment. The City will rezone the existing limited 
Industrial, Limited Industrial-Transit, and Planned Community zoning districts in the Precise 
Plan area into the new Precise Plan zoning district with associated standards and guidelines. The 
City will also study the potential rezoning or inclusion in the Precise Plan area of the Village 
Center commercial area. 




• Precise Plan Transportation Activities. If adopted, the Precise Plan would: 
File No. A-J 6-139 
Page No. 3 
o Incorporate parking standards, complete streets planning, streetscape design standards, 
and multi-modal transportation planning; 
o Reduce peak-hour vehicle trips through the development of Transportation Demand 
Management strategies, regular monitoring, reporting, and penalties for noncompliance; 
o Improve access by coordinating pedestrian and vehicle circulation networks, increase 
pedestrian connections, and identify and improve bike connections; 
o Promote greater connections to the Valley Transportation Authority light rail and Caltrain 
stations; 
o Provide new parking strategies and requirements to increase viability of commercial 
services and reevaluate office parking needs; and 
o Create a list of priority-off-site improvements in the area. 
• Other Infrastructure Activities. The Precise Plan may include the expansion of the City's 
recycled water system to the Precise Plan area. 
The Precise Plan also contemplates consideration of a mixed-use Village Center that would 
revitalize a 10-acre commercial area in the southwestern portion of the Precise Plan area, 1,194 feet 
southeast from the Commissioner's residence. According to the City, however, there are currently 
no formal plans for development of the Village Center area; the parcels that comprise the Village 
Center area are owned by various owners; and it is unlikely that the development of the Village 
Center area would impact the intensity of use of the properties surrounding the Commissioner's 
residence. 
The western portion of the Precise Plan area closest to the Commissioner's residence, 
however, is a Superfund site, an environmentally polluted area where no residential building is 
permitted, so only commercial buildings could be authorized there. And there are no pending 
development applications for higher density commercial development within that western portion of 
the Precise Plan area. 
The Commissioner owns her primary residence, a condominium within a condominium 
complex in a large, built-out residential neighborhood, located 1,005 feet west of North Whisman 
Road, the nearest boundary of the Precise Plan area. The condominium complex is located in a cu I­
de-sac at the western end of Walker Drive, a small, lightly travelled street that primarily serves a 
small portion of a larger residential area. Through traffic into and out of the Precise Plan area is not 
possible on Walker Drive because the eastern end of Walker Drive tenninates at North Whisman 
Road. And the City does not anticipate that adoption of the Precise Plan would have any direct 
effect on the traffic on Walker Drive. 
The Commissioner's condominium has an attached garage, and her condominium complex 
has ample on- and off-street guest parking; the condominium is two stories high, and the 
Commissioner cannot see any portion of the Precise Plan area from any window within; and the 
condominium is located in one of the noisier areas of the City due to being nearby to Moffet Field, 
a military airbase, and under the flight path of military aircraft frequently landing and taking off. 




ANALYSIS 
File No. A-16-139 
Page No. 4 
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Under Section 87 l 03(b ), an official has a financial interest in a decision within 
the meaning of Section 87 100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth 
$2,000 or more. 
With respect to the decisions at issue, the Commissioner has a real property financial 
interest in her residence under the Act. Therefore, we must determine whether the financial effect of 
the decisions at issue on the Commissioner's real property interest in her residence is both 
foreseeable and material. 
Foreseeability & Materiality 
For an interest such as the Commissioner's residence, which is not explicitly involved2 in 
the decision, Regulation 18701 (b) provides that the financial effect of a decision on an official's 
financial interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more 
than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Regulation l 8702.2(a) sets forth the standards for determining whether the reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect of a decision on a public official's real property interest is material. 
Relevant to your inquiry and the facts presented, the financial effect on an official's real property 
interest is material if the decision: 
• 
Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering 
traflic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the 
official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including 
odors, or any other factors that would afect the market value of the real property 
parcel in which the official has a financial interest (Regulation I 8702.2(a)( I 0)); or 
• 
Would cause a reasonably prudent person using due care and consideration under the 
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its 
reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official"s 
property. (Regulation l8702.2(a)(l2).) 
Traffic Levels 
The proposed General Plan amendment and Precise Plan adoption would authorize within 
the Precise Plan area the addition of residential uses, an increase in the permitted office floor area 
ratio, and an increase in the permitted intensity of commercial uses. Over time, with ensuing project 
approvals, these proposed authorizations are likely to result in additional vehicular trips and 
changing peak vehicular use times within the Precise Plan area. 
 An interest is explicitly involved in a deciston if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
governmental decision. or if the decision is identified in Regulation l 8702.2(a)( 1 )-(6). (Regulation l 870l(a).) 


. 


File No. A-16-I 39 
Page No. 5 
The Precise Plan, however, contains mitigation measures designed to minimize any traffic 
increases resultant from new development within the Precise Plan area. Among other things, the 
proposed Precise Plan would: require the incorporation of parking standards, comprehensive street 
planning, and multi-modal transportation planning; require the implementation of Transportation 
Demand Management strategies to reduce peak-hour vehicle trips, including regular monitoring, 
reporting, and penalties for noncompliance; and encourage the use of alternatives to motor-vehicle 
transit, such as walking, biking, and light rail. 
The Commissioner's residence is a condominium within a condominium complex located 
1,005 feet from the Precise Plan area in a large, built-out residential area. The condominium 
complex is located in a cul-de-sac at the western end of Walker Drive, a small, lightly travelled 
street that primarily serves a small portion of the larger residential area. Through traffic into and out 
of the Precise Plan area is not possible on Walker Drive because the eastern end of Walker Drive 
terminates at North Whisman Road. Because through traffic into and out of the Precise Plan area is 
not possible on Walker Drive, the General Plan amendment and Precise Plan adoption arc unlikely 
to result in a significant increase in traffic on Walker Drive, especially with respect to its western 
end in closest proximity to the Commissioner's residence. Consistent with this analysis, the City 
does not anticipate that the adoption of the Precise Plan would have any direct effect on the traffic 
on Walker Drive. 
Thus, we find that the proposed General Plan amendment and Precise Plan adoption would 
not change the character of the Commissioner's residence by substantially altering traffic levels of 
the properties surrounding it. 
Intensity of Use 
The City estimates that approximately 5,000 multi-family units could be added throughout 
the 403-acre Precise Plan area, and anticipates that increased commercial floor area ratio within the 
Precise Plan area will result in higher density commercial development there, over time. 
The western portion of the Precise Plan area closest to the Commissioner's residence, 
however, is a Superfund site, so only commercial buildings could be authorized there. And there are 
no pending development applications for higher density commercial development within that 
western portion of the Precise Plan area. 
The Precise Plan also contemplates consideration of a mixed-use Village Center that would 
revitalize a I 0-acre commercial area in the southwestern portion of the Precise Plan area 1,194 feet 
southeast from the Commissioner's residence. However, there are currently no formal plans for the 
development of the Village Center area. The parcels that comprise the Village Center area are 
owned by various owners, and this could pose challenges to the viability of developing it. And 
although this area could be developed into a more intense mixed land use if the City amends its 
General Plan and adopts the Precise Plan, the City states that is it unlikely that the development of 
the Village Center area would impact the intensity of use of the properties surrounding the 
Commissioner's residence. 
Furthermore, as noted above, the Commissioner's residence is within a large, built-out 
residential neighborhood in a condominium complex at the end of a cul-de-sac, 1,005 feet from the 


. 


File No. A-16-139 
Page No. 6 
nearest boundary to the Precise Plan area, naturally buffered from the potential externalities 
resultant from the authorization of residential and higher-density commercial uses therein. 
Therefore, the decisions at issue are unlikely to substantially alter the privacy of the 
Commissioner's residence or the properties surrounding it. 
The Commissioner's condominium has an attached garage, her condominium complex has 
ample on- and off-street guest parking available, and her condominium is 1,005 feet from the 
Precise Plan area, so the decisions at issue are unlikely to substantially alter the parking available 
for her residence or the properties surrounding it. 
With respect to the potential effect of the decisions at issue on the Commissioner's view 
from her residence, the Commissioner's condominium is two stories high, and she cannot see any 
portion of the Precise Plan area from any window within. Therefore, the decisions are unlikely to 
substantially alter the view from her residence or that of surrounding properties. 
The Commissioner's residence is located within one of the noisier portions of the City due 
to being nearby to Moffett Field, a military air base, and under the flight path of military aircraft 
frequently landing and taking off. Although the decisions at issue could result in intennittent 
increased noise fonn construction of ensuing development projects, this additional noise would be 
miniscule in comparison to the existing noise from Moffett Field. Thus, the decisions are unlikely to 
substantially alter noise levels for the Commissioner's residence or those of surrounding properties. 
With respect to the potential effect of the decisions at issue on the air quality of the 
Commissioner's residence, the authorization of residential uses and higher density commercial uses 
may result in increased dust or odors from construction activities and additional vehicle exhaust. 
However, the potential construction impacts would be temporary, and any resultant vehicle exhaust 
would be minimized by the proposed Precise Plan's traffic mitigation measures. In addition, the 
Commissioner's residence and the surrounding properties are a significant distance from the Precise 
Plan area, within a built-out residential neighborhood buffered from these potential externalities. 
Therefore, we find that the proposed General Plan amendment and Precise Plan adoption 
would not change the character of the Commissioner's residence by substantially altering the 
intensity of use of the properties surrounding it. 
Affect or Influence the Market Value of the Commissioner's Residence? 
As noted above, the General Plan amendment and Precise Plan adoption would authorize 
residential uses and higher intensity commercial uses within the Precise Plan area. The decisions at 
issue, however, would not change the character of the Commissioner's residence by substantially 
altering the traffic levels or intensity of use of properties surrounding the Commissioner's 
residence. And the facts presented do not indicate that the market value of the Commissioner's 
residence would be affected by these decisions in any way. Moreover, even if the City amends the 
General Plan and adopts the Precise Plan, further governmental decisions will be necessary for the 
approval of any ensuing development project. Therefore, we find that the decisions relating to the 
potential General Plan amendment and Precise Plan adoption will not affect or influence the market 
value of the Commissioner's residence. 




File No. A-16-1 39 
Page No. 7 
Accordingly, we conclude that the Act docs not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part 
in governmental decisions relating to the proposed General Plan amendment or Precise Plan 
adoption. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Matt cw . 
ms y 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in governmental decisions relating 
to the City's proposed amendment of its General Plan and adoption of the East Whisman Precise 
Plan given that the Commissioner owns her residence located 1,005 feet from the Precise Plan area?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in those decisions because 
they will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real property financial 
interest in her residence.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in governmental decisions relating 
to the City's proposed amendment of its General Plan and adoption of the East Whisman Precise 
Plan given that the Commissioner owns her residence located 1,005 feet from the Precise Plan area?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in those decisions because 
they will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real property financial 
interest in her residence.

embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION · 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Krishan Chopra Senior Assistant City Attorney Office of the City Attorney P.O. Box 7540 Mountain View, CA 94039-7540 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-139 Dear Mr. Chopra: August 12, 2016 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Mountain View Environmental Planning Commissioner Lisa Matichak regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the ''Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice. (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section I 090. QUESTION Does the Act prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the City's proposed amendment of its General Plan and adoption of the East Whisman Precise Plan given that the Commissioner owns her residence located 1,005 feet from the Precise Plan area? CONCLUSION No. The Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in those decisions because they will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real property financial interest in her residence. 1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Govemmenl Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. FACTS File No. A-16-139 Page No. 2 You are a Senior Assistant City Attorney for the City of Mountain View and the authorized representative of Environmental Planning Commissioner Lisa Matichak. The City will soon consider whether to amend its General Plan and whether to adopt the East Whisman Precise Plan. The East Whisman Precise Plan (the "Precise Plan") is a planning document that would modify zoning and design standards for future development within the East Whisman Change Area (the "Precise Plan area"), an area identified in the City's existing General Plan. The Precise Plan area is approximately 403 acres in size. The existing land uses within the Precise Plan area are primarily office and light industrial, with scattered commercial and retail uses. The City's recently adopted General Plan authorized an increase of 1.1 million net new square feet of commercial office and 27,000 square feet of retail uses, but did not authorize any residential uses, within the proposed Precise Plan area. The proposed General Plan amendment and Precise Plan adoption would authorize within the Precise Plan area the addition of residential uses, an increase in the permitted office "floor area
